title: Chilli con carne
subtitle: Beef and kidney beans slow-cooked with chilli and spices.
excerpt: Beef mince browned with onion and garlic, simmered with chilli, cumin, smoked paprika, kidney beans and tomatoes until thick and deeply flavoured. Serve with rice, soured cream and coriander. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Chilli con carne. The dish originates in the American Southwest, specifically in Texas, where it developed from Mexican-American cooking traditions in the 19th century. The first known written recipe appears in William Gebhardt's publications in the late 1890s. The version made in British and American home kitchens with mince, kidney beans and tinned tomatoes became standardised through the mid-20th century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to three months. Defrost overnight; reheat on the hob with a splash of water.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles and triples well. An ideal make-ahead and freeze dish.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better made the day before; the flavour improves overnight as the spices settle.

content.text: The difference between chilli that tastes of mince with spices and chilli that has real depth is time and browning. The mince must be browned properly in a hot, uncrowded pan, not steamed in its own juice. The onions must go long enough to develop sweetness. The spices need two minutes in the fat before the liquid goes in. After that, the work is just patience: 45 minutes of low simmering.

items.prepNote: a small square; optional for depth

content.text: Heat {{rapeseed-oil}} of oil in a large casserole over high heat. Add {{beef-mince}} of mince in two or three batches, leaving space between pieces. Cook without stirring for 3 minutes per batch until deeply browned, then break up. Transfer browned mince to a bowl.

Reduce the heat to medium. Cook {{onion}} diced onions in the same pan for 10 minutes until softened and beginning to colour. Add {{garlic}} of chopped garlic and cook 2 minutes. Add {{chilli-powder}} of chilli powder, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, {{coriander-ground}} of coriander and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée; stir and cook for 2 minutes until fragrant.

Return the browned mince to the pan. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{kidney-beans}} of kidney beans and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a simmer, then cook on low heat for 40 to 45 minutes until the sauce is thick and the meat is tender. In the last 5 minutes, stir in {{dark-chocolate}} of dark chocolate if using; it deepens the flavour without adding sweetness.

items.cause: Mince steamed rather than browned, or spices added without cooking them out.
items.fix: Brown the mince in batches; crowding the pan causes steaming. Cook the dry spices in the fat for 2 minutes before adding liquid.

items.cause: Over-seasoned with chilli powder, which varies widely in heat.
items.fix: Taste before the beans go in and adjust. Stir in a spoonful of soured cream to cool a batch that's too hot.

items.cause: Lid on during simmering, trapping steam.
items.fix: Simmer uncovered or with the lid slightly ajar. The chilli should reduce to a thick, coating consistency.

content.text: Chilli is the weeknight staple of the British as much as the American home. It is forgiving, cheap, better the second day, and serves cheerfully from a pot on the hob without ceremony. Serve over rice with soured cream, or in baked potatoes, or on nachos if it's that kind of evening.
